dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a fall reported at 3483 East Clay Streets.
Hi, I'm Randy, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Uh, I fell while trying to get out of bed. My neck is hurting a bit.
Can you open your eyes for me, please?
What is your name?
Janet Martin.
Can you tell me where you are right now?
I'm in my bedroom.
Can you squeeze my fingers with both hands?
Sure.
Patient is fully alert and oriented to person, place, and time.
Is your throat sore or hard to breathe through?
No, breathing is fine.
Patient's chest movement is equal on both sides.
Respirations are unlabored and regular.
Pulse is strong and regular on both arms.
Skin is warm, pink, and dry. 18. History of Present Illness; sample:_sign_symptoms; EMT: Can you tell me when the pain in your neck started?

I've been having that pain for a few days now. 20. History of Present Illness; sample:_sign_symptoms; EMT: Can you describe the pain? Is it sharp or dull? 21. History of Present Illness; sample:_sign_symptoms; Patient: It's a dull ache. 22. Pain Assessment; opqrst:_severity; EMT: On a scale from 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst, how bad is your neck pain? 23. Pain Assessment; opqrst:_severity; Patient: It's about a 5. 24. History of Present Illness; sample:_past_medical_history; EMT: Do you have any medical history or pre-existing conditions? 25. History of Present Illness; sample:_past_medical_history; Patient: I have heart failure and a history of falls.
Any known drug allergies? 27. History of Present Illness; sample:_allergies; Patient: I don't have any known allergies.
What medications are you currently taking? 29. History of Present Illness; sample:_current_medications; Patient: Simvastatin, atenolol, Lasix, and a few others for my thyroid and heart.
Can you tell me what happened when you fell? 31. History of Present Illness; sample:_events; Patient: I tried to get up, felt dizzy, and then I fell.
When you fell, did you lose consciousness?
No, I was awake the whole time.
Your skin is warm, pink, and dry. No rashes or wounds noted.
No bruising or unusual rashes observed.
Your eyes are PERRL-equal, round, reactive.
Your nose and mouth are clear with no signs of bleeding.
You mentioned pain, but is there any swelling or deformity in your neck?
No, just the pain.
No deformities, contusions, abrasions, or penetrating injuries of the neck observed.
Your chest moves equally.
Chest rise is equal on both sides. Lung sounds are clear in all lobes.
No deformities, contusions, abrasions, or penetrating injuries of the chest observed.
Your abdomen is soft and non-tender.
No deformities, contusions, abrasions, or penetrating injuries of the abdomen observed.
Have you had any pain or discomfort in your back?
No, just my neck.
No deformities, contusions, abrasions, or penetrating injuries of the back observed.
Pulse in your arms and legs is strong and regular.
Your pulse is 70 beats per minute.
Your respiratory rate is 20 breaths per minute.
Blood pressure is 109 over 81 millimeters of mercury.
Your blood glucose is 105 milligrams per deciliter.
Your oxygen saturation is 99 percent.
No jugular vein distention observed.
Would you allow us to place a cervical collar to stabilize your neck?
I guess that is okay.
Cervical collar applied successfully after one attempt.
Your pulse is 63 now.
Blood pressure is 123 over 61.
Blood glucose is undetectable using the device.
SpO2 is 98 percent.
We'll need to send down your heart rhythm using the monitor.
Four lead EKG is in place and monitoring.
12-lead ECG obtained, findings are normal.
Your pulse now is 60.
Blood pressure is 129 over 61.
SpO2 remains at 99 percent.
No jugular vein distention or tracheal deviation noted.
Because of your age and fall with neck pain, we are following the spinal motion restriction protocol.
I'd like to know, which hospital would you prefer for treatment?
Wherever you take me is fine.
We'll transport you to St. Mary's Hospital.
We'll be moving you down the stairs on a stair chair and then onto a stretcher.
We'll transfer you to the wheelchair next and give you full support.
All seat belts have been attached to the stretcher.
We have you strapped in with 5-point harness for transport safety.
Keeper, we are en route with the patient to St. Mary's.
St. Mary's is aware and will be prepared.
EKG is still active and monitoring.
No jugular vein distention seen.
IV start attempted in an extremity vein (arm or leg), unsuccessful after one attempt.
Patient is showing no change in character or responsiveness observed.
We're transferring you over to the ED bed using a sheet lift.
We'll hand over your care to the ED nurse upon arrival.
Crossing into St. Mary's. Prepare contact with receiving nurse.
Transfer to ED bed is complete.
Everything has shifted over to ED care now.
Patient was transported to the hospital while on the stretcher in a cold condition, with all seat belts attached.
Ready to load the patient into the ambulance.
Patient safely loaded into the ambulance.
Doing final status check: pulse 60, BP 129/61, SpO2 99, GCS 15.
All vital signs are within normal limits; en route to St. Mary's.
All on board for St. Mary's heading northbound. Keep an eye on those rear doors.
No jugular vein distention or tracheal deviation noted.
You mentioned dizziness and a fall-do you also feel any weakness in your limbs or body?
I didn't notice any weakness.

You're taking several medications for your heart and thyroid. That includes simvastatin (Vytorin), atenolol (Tenormin), spironolactone (Aldactazide), and carvedilol (Coreg). You also take allopurinol (Aloprim), Lasix, levothyroxine (Synthroid), and lorazepam (Ativan).
Unable to print a patient care report due to high call volume.
